# What Does It Mean To Heal?

### In a worldview where mind and body are connected, an imbalance in either reflects in the other. Aligning both leads to healing but also much else.

[](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

![Garima Garg's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!h9ms!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2493377a-60dd-44d9-a41e-4aff3778a15c_660x1006.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!QGkI!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fba7a6530-f9f5-4c54-b3c9-c3cba490c693_1827x1083.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!QGkI!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fba7a6530-f9f5-4c54-b3c9-c3cba490c693_1827x1083.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*Tulips at Phipps Conservatory and Botanical Gardens, Pittsburgh, in 2016. Photo by Garima Garg.*

“Fairy tales do not tell children the dragons exist. Children already know that dragons exist. Fairy tales tell children the dragons can be killed”, is what G.K. Chesterton, the British conservative, once expressed in his book, *Tremendous Trifles*. This popular quote is actually a paraphrased version of the original but conveys the essence of his insight about why we need stories, especially when we are children.

While all of us deal with dragons throughout our lives, not all of us manage to kill them off because we do not know that they can be killed. Most of us do not grow up reading fairy tales which can give us that courage, clarity, and conviction. But anyone who has ever deal with a dragon as a child and grown up to vanquish it will attest to the boundless optimism of this wonderful quote. Despite their differing colours and forms, all of our dragons may be characterised as— life is frighteningly unfair, the world is an endlessly tough place, and no one can help us get through the bridge we alone must cross. A dragon can show up in our lives out of nowhere and for no reason, start to breathe fire upon us. This fire, like the dragon, is extremely real and has the power to burn away our essence entirely. Yet, if we believe that the dragon can be vanquished, we can look for a weapon that may have arrived along with the creature (it always does). If we have the weapon, we may summon the willpower to use it to our advantage (we can always do that). If we have the willpower, we may just persist long enough until the dragon is dead (we do end up doing just that). And once it is dead, we can finally breathe once again but with a lot more calm (just as inevitable as a sunrise).

One of the leading dragons of the world, cancer, actually may lead us to this very insight for ourselves too. Cancer confounds doctors and commoners alike for its unique nature as an illness. It is, after all, not a foreign agent attacking our body but our own cells gone rogue. When bad cells in the body start multiplying rapidly in any part of our body, they not only start killing off the good ones but are also able to evade the watchful gaze of our immune system which does not turn on in time to fend off these bad cells. In other words, cancer is aggravated, if not caused, when our immune function fails to do its job. So, why does that happen? Researchers today give us many answers— genetic predispositions, environmental toxins, lack of enough sleep, unhealthy food, deep emotional issues, and even stress.

No wonder then, that modern discourse around health is becoming increasingly alarmist with random influencers waxing eloquent about the sympathetic nervous system which keeps us in a constant fight-or-flight mode due to our stressful lifestyles. In order for us to be calm, their 2-min videos (or 2-hour long meandering podcasts) tell us that we need to engage our parasympathetic nervous system with practises like mindfulness, yoga, slow and intentional lifestyles, eating and sleeping healthier.

The key difference in these two modes of nervous system is that when our body is in a state of stress, it pumps a lot of cortisol which prepares us to deal with a challenging situation. But if it stays in a state of chronic stress, it leads to too much cortisol and not enough of the calmer hormones such as oxytocin, serotonin, progesterone, and prolactin. While that helps in our fast-paced over-achieving world, it is not so great for our bodies. When we cannot relax, our immune function cannot undertake its regular rest, repair, and regenerative work. Eventually, the initial signs of our health going bad is inflammation, weight gain due to insulin resistance, and faster wear and tear such as greying of hair, dull skin, and so on.

In other words, the human body is set up like a stack of dominos. When one falls, it can lead to more dominos falling. The good news is that the body is incredibly resilient. In his book, *The Beautiful Cure*, Daniel Davis points out that 95% of our immunity is innate. That means, it does not need any medicine in a healthy body to keep itself functioning. That is staggering because the modern medical consumerism dictates we must ingest as many supplements as we can to keep ourselves healthy. Yet, nothing could be further from truth than that. It is only in following a routine that ensure our physical, emotional, and mental well-being that we start to take control of our health in the best possible way.

But even if you do that, what to do about the fact that everything around us is toxic— air, food, water, detergents, plastic products, cosmetics, perfumes, electronic devices, and so on and so forth. Our screens, for instance, increase our cortisol and decrease our brain’s ability to make melatonin, the sleep hormone. We cannot go back to a world without screens, at least not entirely. So how is anyone to find any healing in such a world? Natural therapies such as forest therapy, sound therapy, aroma therapy, and even colour therapy, are now becoming popular once again but they remain a piece of a puzzle, not the solution.

The spiritual world says to this— what if the dragon is not just something that exists outside of us but within us as well? After all, that is precisely the nature of cancer. While doctors and researchers express optimism in following a healthy lifestyle, regular medical check-ups to catch a diagnosis as early as possible if ever it comes to pass, and maintaining emotional well-being, many people still do end up facing the terrible fate of a harsh cancer treatment. Because for one, we can never agree on what is healthy. For Westerners, raw salads and milk are the epitome of health but for Indians used to Ayurvedic principles, no vegetable should ever be consumed raw and no food should be combined with milk. Secondly, the medical community is divided on the effectiveness of a cancer treatment. While many patients end up having to go through repeated relapses, there are also quite a few who recover on their own. Indeed, there have been [documented cases](https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20150306-the-mystery-of-vanishing-cancer) of what is known as a spontaneous remission where even an advanced stage cancer ends up healing itself. Caroline Myss, in her book, *Anatomy of the Spirit*, mentions a lot more such cases of people healing illnesses such as cancer, AIDS, chronic body pain and the like, simply by addressing their emotional and spiritual conflicts. At the same time, she also mentions cases where an illness held its sway over an individual despite their robust emotional and spiritual health. An illness, then, is an opportunity to go inwards and clear out some baggage. This act of cleaning-up may help us heal but it’s not the cure itself either.

Which brings us to the same question again— how are we to find healing in this world, especially one that lasts? Hindu philosophy tells us that the existence is like a play, or Krishna’s leela, or a dance of Shiva. The gods will this dance into existence, with all its beauty and violence, and one day, will it into destruction and oblivion. They keep on doing this for an inconceivable eternity of which we witness but a moment in a lifetime. But just as the gods and their dance, our souls too manifest infinitely. In other words, we go through this cycle of birth and death for an eternity too. In this context, the prospect of a meaningful healing is pretty much impossible.

Yet, what if there is more to this story still? What happens when we learn to vanquish our dragons? We become free of the fear of the dragon. Meeting and overcoming an adversity calmly makes us braver to be our true selves and live out our lives with more joy, warmth, and depth. Being able to actualise all that is good in us further reinforces all that is good in the world. After all, this is the single greatest advantage we have as human beings in comparison to our non-human counterparts. While all species are engaged in a battle of survival, only human beings have the discernment to go beyond it and build functional societies and civilisations. Once again, our structures do not last forever but what lasts is our all-too-human attempt itself at building anything worthwhile. This is not just about controlling our animalistic instincts but also about realising and manifesting our divine instincts. That we get to do this for an eternity may be a drag but can also be a blessing. Perhaps, that is all the healing that we can ever truly have (or need).
